evidence_quotes.quote: Many video games require online access to be played.

Blizzard reserves the right to terminate this Agreement at any time for any reason, or for no reason, with or without notice to you.

In the event of a termination of this Agreement, any right you may have had to any pre-purchased Game access or virtual goods, such as digital cards, currency, weapons, armor, wearable items, skins, sprays, pets, mounts, etc., are forfeit, and you agree and acknowledge that you are not entitled to any refund for any amounts which were pre-paid on your Account prior to any termination of this Agreement.

paragraphs.text: To quote a End-User-Licence Agreement of one of the biggest Videogame publishers on Earth: "Blizzard reserves the right to terminate this Agreement at any time for any reason, or for no reason, with or without notice to you. [...] In the event of a termination of this Agreement, any right you may have had to any pre-purchased Game access or virtual goods, such as digital cards, currency, weapons, armor, wearable items, skins, sprays, pets, mounts, etc., are forfeit, and you agree and acknowledge that you are not entitled to any refund for any amounts which were pre-paid on your Account prior to any termination of this Agreement." To translate what they are saying: They can, will and already have taken away access to Games from consumers. Consumers, which paid up to 70 (For a standart copy of a Game. In excess of 200 for the fanciest versions) Nowhere on the Store Page is this Information publicly displayed. And the wording "Purchase" and "Buy" is used. When i "Buy" something. I expect to own it. I expect to be able to do anything with it. And i expect to be able to keep using it as long as i want. I do not expect to get a licence from a Company to be able to use a product as long as the Company wants me to use it, with NO pre-defined expiration date, NO compensation when my access inevitibly WILL be revoked. And no alternative way to retain access after the Company takes the licence away from me. Many video games require online access to be played. If this online access is no longer available, because the publisher of the game decided, they don't want to support it anymore, then my copy of a game gets effectivly destroyed. It is unreasonable to assume that these publishers can keep online service running indefintily. There are costs associated with that, and indefinite support is basically impossible. Nobody is asking for that. What is reasonable to expect are some or all of the below: - a predefined Expiration Date for a Game - a predefined end-of-life plan for a Game, what happens to it after the publisher no longer supports it - a way that makes it possible to keep a Game running for consumers, even if at their own expense and risk. This would have to be provided for by the publisher, since they hold the rights to run these online services. - a game structure that allows continued function (even if to a limited extent) of the game, after end-of-life. There is a need for regulation in this space, because if there isn't, Millions of people will be essentially robbed of things they legally bought.
